content:
# Leviticus Chapter 17 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 68r–68v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_017_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_017_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 103–104

---

**1** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**2** "Speak to Aaron and to his sons and to all the sons of Israel, and say to them: This is the word that the LORD has commanded:

**3** Any man of the house of Israel who slaughters an ox or a lamb or a goat in the camp, or who slaughters it outside the camp,

**4** and does not bring it to the entrance of the tent of meeting to offer it as an offering to the LORD before the tabernacle of the LORD — bloodguilt shall be reckoned to that man; he has shed blood, and that man shall be cut off from among his people.

**5** This is so that the sons of Israel may bring their sacrifices that they sacrifice in the open field — that they may bring them to the LORD, to the entrance of the tent of meeting, to the priest, and sacrifice them as peace offerings to the LORD.

**6** And the priest shall throw the blood against the altar of the LORD at the entrance of the tent of meeting, and burn the fat for a pleasing aroma to the LORD.

**7** So they shall no longer sacrifice their sacrifices to the goat-demons [se'irim], after whom they are playing the harlot. This shall be a statute forever for them throughout their generations.

**8** "And you shall say to them: Any man of the house of Israel, or of the sojourners who sojourn among them, who offers a burnt offering or a sacrifice,

**9** and does not bring it to the entrance of the tent of meeting to offer it to the LORD — that man shall be cut off from his people.

**10** "And any man of the house of Israel, or of the sojourners who sojourn among them, who eats any blood — I will set my face against that person who eats blood, and will cut him off from among his people.

**11** For the life [nefesh] of the flesh is in the blood, and I have given it to you on the altar to make atonement for your lives [nafshoteikhem]; for it is the blood that makes atonement by the life [ba-nefesh].

**12** Therefore I have said to the sons of Israel: No person among you shall eat blood, nor shall any sojourner who sojourns among you eat blood.

**13** "Any man of the sons of Israel, or of the sojourners who sojourn among them, who hunts and catches game — beast or bird that may be eaten — he shall pour out its blood and cover it with dust.

**14** For the life [nefesh] of every creature is its blood — its blood is its life [be-nafsho]. Therefore I have said to the sons of Israel: You shall not eat the blood of any creature, for the life of every creature is its blood. Whoever eats it shall be cut off.

**15** And every person, whether native or sojourner, who eats what has died naturally or what was torn by beasts, shall wash his clothes and bathe himself in water and be unclean until the evening; then he shall be clean.

**16** But if he does not wash or bathe his body, he shall bear his iniquity."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations

**Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 68r–68v:**
Leviticus 17 follows immediately upon the Day of Atonement legislation (ch. 16), forming a sharp pivot in the Holiness Code. The chapter is brief — only 16 verses — but carries a density of theological weight disproportionate to its length. The Leningrad Codex folios 68r and 68v are well preserved at this point, with legible three-column text and Masorah parva annotations in the margins. The chapter begins mid-folio on 68r and concludes on 68v, which then continues into Leviticus 18. The text shows no significant anomalies in the MT tradition at this location. The chapter divides clearly into three units marked by the repeated address formula "Any man of the house of Israel... or of the sojourners who sojourn among them" (vv. 3, 8, 10, 13), creating a legal code applicable to both native Israelites and resident aliens.

The chapter functions as a transition point between the two major structural divisions of Leviticus: the ritual legislation of chapters 1–16 (the Priestly Code proper) and the Holiness Code of chapters 17–26 (identified by scholars by the refrain "be holy, for I the LORD your God am holy"). Most critical scholars identify a compositional seam here; the MT's final form unites the two blocks through the common thread of blood theology.

**Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 103–104:**
Leviticus 17 appears in the Vaticanus across pages 103 (right-hand portion) and 104 (left-hand section, marked CAP VI in the LXX chapter numeration). The LXX of Leviticus 17 shows close alignment with the MT structure but contains several notable variants. The key term se'irim (goat-demons) is rendered μάταια (mataia, "vain/empty things / idols") in Vaticanus — a striking interpretive flattening that eliminates the demonic connotation of the Hebrew. The blood-life formula of v. 11 is handled carefully in the Greek with ψυχή (psyche) used consistently for nefesh. The phrase "cut off from among his people" is rendered ἐξολεθρευθήσεται (exolethreuthēsetai, "shall be utterly destroyed"), a Greek term that often intensifies the MT's karet formula.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. "Bloodguilt shall be reckoned to that man" (v. 4) — dam yechashev la-ish ha-hu:**
The verb chashav (Niphal, "shall be reckoned, imputed, counted") applied to dam ("blood") creates a forensic declaration: field slaughter without proper ritual presentation is treated legally as homicide. The phrase is rendered "bloodguilt shall be reckoned to that man" rather than the weaker "he will be guilty of bloodshed" (NIV) or "blood is imputed to that man" (KJV). The term "bloodguilt" captures the compound semantic field: culpability for an act of blood that violates sacred boundaries. The reasoning in v. 4 is theological — slaughter outside the tent deprives the blood of its proper ritual destination (the altar), thereby treating the animal's life as if it had no sacred value. The continuation "he has shed blood" (dam shafakh) underscores that unauthorized slaughter is structurally equated with murder in the Priestly legal imagination.

**2. "Peace offerings" (v. 5) — zevach shelamim:**
The term shelamim (from shalom, "wholeness, completeness, peace") denotes the category of shared sacrifices in which portions are given to God (burned on the altar), to the priests (breast and right thigh), and to the offerer (the remainder). The standard LOGOS rendering "peace offerings" is retained, following ESV/NASB/KJV tradition. Some scholars prefer "fellowship offerings" (NIV) to emphasize the communal dimension, or "well-being offerings" (NRSV) to avoid the potentially narrow English sense of "peace" as mere absence of conflict. The formal equivalence tradition preserves "peace offerings" because shelamim is a technical terminus technicus with a precise ritual definition; "peace" carries the full resonance of shalom (wholeness, completeness, right relationship).

**3. "Goat-demons" (v. 7) — se'irim:**
This is among the most significant terms in the chapter. Se'irim (singular: sa'ir) literally means "hairy ones" or "shaggy ones" — the same word used for the male goat (sa'ir) in the sacrificial code. In this context (la-se'irim, "to the goat-demons"), the term refers to demonic entities of the wilderness to whom illicit sacrifices were being offered. The parallel in 2 Chronicles 11:15 (where Jeroboam appoints priests "for the goat-demons and for the calves he had made") confirms the demonic sense. Isaiah 13:21 and 34:14 use se'irim for wilderness creatures associated with desolation and demonic occupation. The term is rendered "goat-demons [se'irim]" with a bracketed transliteration, preserving both the demonic force and the Hebrew root. The LXX Vaticanus renders μάταια (mataia, "vain/futile things" — an abstraction), collapsing the specific demonic reference into a general polemic against idols. This translation follows the MT's more specific and startling reference.

The command "after whom they are playing the harlot" (asher hem zonim achareihem) uses the verb zanah (Qal participle), the standard prophetic idiom for religious apostasy as spiritual adultery. It is rendered literally: "after whom they are playing the harlot." The fornication metaphor implies covenant betrayal — Israel's exclusive covenant with YHWH is violated by offering sacrifices to other beings.

**4. "The life of the flesh is in the blood" (v. 11) — ki nefesh ha-basar ba-dam hi:**
This is the theological apex of the chapter and one of the most consequential sentences in the entire Pentateuch for later Jewish and Christian theology of atonement. Three issues require attention:

(a) *Nefesh*: The term nefesh is rendered "life" throughout vv. 11 and 14, following its primary semantic register in this biological/physical context (rather than "soul," which carries anachronistic Platonic connotations). Nefesh in the MT refers to the living being as a whole — the animating vitality that makes flesh alive. The statement "the nefesh of the flesh is in the blood" is a phenomenological observation: blood is the visible carrier of the life-principle.

(b) *"I have given it to you on the altar"* — va-ani netattiv lakhem al-ha-mizbe'ach: The divine first person is fronted with force: "and I — I have given it to you." God is the donor of the atoning blood. This is rendered "and I have given it to you on the altar" with the personal pronoun "I" prominent. The theological implication is that atonement is not a human achievement but a divine provision: God gives the blood-life for the purpose of covering human sin.

(c) *"To make atonement for your lives"* — le-khapper al-nafshoteikhem: The Pi'el of kipper ("to cover, to make atonement") with the preposition al ("over, for, upon") and the direct object nafshoteikhem ("your lives/selves") is the standard atonement formula. The phrase is rendered "to make atonement for your lives" rather than "for your souls" to maintain the physicality and non-dualistic sense of nefesh.

(d) *"By the life"* — ba-nefesh: The concluding phrase ki-ha-dam hu ba-nefesh yekhapper ("for the blood — it is by the life that it makes atonement") is rendered "for it is the blood that makes atonement by the life." The preposition bet ("by/through") with nefesh indicates that the blood makes atonement specifically through/by means of the life it contains. This is not a casual observation but the logical ground of the entire sacrificial system: blood atones because it is the vehicle of life, and life given to God substitutes for and covers the offerer's forfeited life.

**5. "Cut off from among his people" (vv. 4, 9, 10, 14) — ve-nikrat ha-nefesh ha-hi me-ammeha:**
The karet penalty ("cutting off") is the most severe sanction in the Priestly legal code short of capital punishment. Its exact meaning has been debated: does it mean execution by human hand, divine execution (premature death), excision from the covenant community (banishment/excommunication), or post-mortem exclusion from the afterlife? The MT uses the Niphal of karat ("to be cut off") with "from among his people" (me-ammeha or me-amav). This translation renders the phrase consistently as "shall be cut off from among his people" / "shall be cut off from his people," preserving the formal legal idiom without specifying the mechanism. The LXX ἐξολεθρευθήσεται ("shall be utterly destroyed") implies divine execution; the MT is deliberately vague, leaving the mechanism in God's hands.

**6. "Plays the harlot" (v. 7) — zonim achareihem:**
The Qal participle zonim (from zanah, "to commit fornication/harlotry") with the directional preposition achareihem ("after them") is the standard prophetic metaphor for covenant apostasy. It is rendered "playing the harlot after" rather than "whoring after" (ESV) or "prostituting themselves to" (NIV) to preserve the formal register appropriate to this legal text while maintaining the force of the metaphor. The term implies deliberate, chosen covenant betrayal: Israel was actively pursuing illicit allegiances.

**7. "What has died naturally or what was torn" (v. 15) — nevelah u-treifah:**
Two distinct categories of improperly-killed animals: nevelah (an animal that died of itself, without proper slaughter — "what has died naturally," literally "a fallen thing") and treifah (an animal torn/killed by predators — from taraf, "to tear"). The term treifah is the origin of the later Hebrew/Yiddish "treyf" for non-kosher food generally. Both terms are rendered by their definitions rather than transliterated, because this is their first conjunction and the categories are straightforwardly described. "What has died naturally" for nevelah is standard ESV/NASB rendering; "what was torn by beasts" for treifah is explicit about the agent. Eating such animals incurs impurity (vv. 15–16) but not the karet penalty, indicating a less severe violation than eating blood.

**8. "Shall bear his iniquity" (v. 16) — nasa avono:**
The phrase nasa avon ("to carry/bear iniquity") is the standard formulation for personal culpability that remains upon an individual who has failed to perform the required purification. It is distinguished from the karet penalty: the person who neglects washing bears ongoing guilt but is not excised from the community. "Bear his iniquity" is the consistent LOGOS rendering throughout Leviticus (cf. chs. 5, 7, 19, 20, 22) for this formula.

**9. "Native" and "sojourner" (vv. 8, 10, 12, 13, 15):**
The chapter addresses both ha-ezrach (the native-born Israelite, "the native") and ha-ger ha-gar be-tokham (the sojourner who sojourns among them). The chapter applies the blood prohibition equally to both — a significant datum for the chapter's theology: the holiness of blood is not ethnically particular but universally binding on all who live within the covenant community's bounds. "Native" for ezrach and "sojourner" for ger are consistent LOGOS renderings throughout Leviticus.

**10. "A statute forever" (v. 7) — chuqqat olam:**
The permanent-statutory formula chuqqat olam ("a statute forever") appears at v. 7, cementing the prohibition against goat-demon sacrifice as a perpetual ordinance. The rendering "a statute forever" follows the established LOGOS pattern (cf. Lev 16:29, 31, 34). The formula marks the transition from narrative-legal prescription to permanent-statutory declaration.

---

### LXX Vaticanus Variants

| Verse | MT (Hebrew) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Note |
|-------|-------------|-----------------|------|
| 4 | dam yechashev la-ish ha-hu ("bloodguilt shall be reckoned to that man") | αἷμα λογισθήσεται τῷ ἀνθρώπῳ ἐκείνῳ | Exact equivalence; αἷμα = dam, λογισθήσεται = yechashev (Niphal future) |
| 4 | ve-nikrat ha-ish ha-hu me-ammo ("that man shall be cut off from his people") | ἐξολεθρευθήσεται ἡ ψυχὴ ἐκείνη ἐκ τοῦ λαοῦ αὐτῆς | LXX intensifies: "shall be utterly destroyed" (ἐξολεθρεύω); adds ψυχή where MT has ha-ish |
| 5 | zevach shelamim ("peace offerings") | θυσίαν σωτηρίου | LXX: "sacrifice of salvation/deliverance" — σωτήριον for shelamim; captures a different semantic nuance (wholeness → salvation) |
| 7 | la-se'irim ("to the goat-demons") | τοῖς ματαίοις | LXX: "to the vain/empty things" — a significant flattening; eliminates demonic connotation and renders as a general polemic against idols; cf. Jer 2:5 LXX use of μάταιος for idols |
| 7 | asher hem zonim achareihem ("after whom they are playing the harlot") | οἷς αὐτοὶ ἐκπορνεύουσιν ὀπίσω αὐτῶν | LXX: ἐκπορνεύω ("to engage in fornication/prostitution after them") — functional equivalence; same metaphor |
| 7 | chuqqat olam ("a statute forever") | νόμιμον αἰώνιον | LXX: νόμιμον ("ordinance/lawful thing") for chuqqat; αἰώνιον for olam — standard equivalents |
| 10 | ve-natatti panai ba-nefesh ha-ochelet et-ha-dam ("I will set my face against that person who eats blood") | ἐπιστήσω τὸ πρόσωπόν μου ἐπὶ τὴν ψυχὴν τὴν ἐσθίουσαν τὸ αἷμα | Exact functional equivalence; ἐπιστήσω = "I will set/direct toward" |
| 11 | nefesh ha-basar ba-dam hi ("the life of the flesh is in the blood") | ἡ γὰρ ψυχὴ πάσης σαρκός αἷμα αὐτοῦ ἐστίν | LXX: "the soul of all flesh — its blood is it"; slightly different syntax; πάσης σαρκός ("of all flesh") anticipates v. 14 |
| 11 | va-ani netattiv lakhem ("and I have given it to you") | κἀγὼ δέδωκα αὐτὸ ὑμῖν | Exact equivalence; κἀγώ (καί + ἐγώ) preserves the emphatic first person |
| 11 | le-khapper al-nafshoteikhem ("to make atonement for your lives") | ἐξιλάσκεσθαι περὶ τῶν ψυχῶν ὑμῶν | LXX: ἐξιλάσκομαι ("to propitiate, to make expiation") for kipper; περί ("concerning, for") for al; exact functional equivalence |
| 11 | ki ha-dam hu ba-nefesh yekhapper ("for it is the blood that makes atonement by the life") | τὸ γὰρ αἷμα αὐτοῦ ἀντὶ τῆς ψυχῆς ἐξιλάσεται | LXX: "for its blood — in exchange for the life — shall make atonement"; ἀντί ("in exchange for, instead of") rather than a beth of means; significant variant: LXX reads the atonement as substitutionary exchange (anti = in place of), while MT's bet may indicate means or agency |
| 13 | ve-shafakh et-damo ve-kissahu be-afar ("he shall pour out its blood and cover it with dust") | ἐκχεεῖ τὸ αἷμα καὶ καλύψει αὐτὸ χοῦν | LXX: χοῦν = "dust/earth" for afar; exact equivalence |
| 14 | ki nefesh kol-basar damo be-nafsho hi ("for the life of every creature is its blood — its blood is its life") | ἡ γὰρ ψυχὴ πάσης σαρκός αἷμα αὐτοῦ ἐστίν | LXX conflates vv. 11 and 14; the formula here matches the LXX rendering of v. 11 almost exactly — the LXX has harmonized the two blood-life statements |
| 15 | nevelah u-treifah ("what has died naturally or what was torn") | θνησιμαῖον ἢ θηριάλωτον | LXX: θνησιμαῖον = "that which died of itself"; θηριάλωτον = "that which was seized/caught by a beast" — functional equivalents that became the standard LXX/patristic terminology for these categories |
| 16 | ve-nasa avono ("he shall bear his iniquity") | καὶ λήμψεται τὴν ἁμαρτίαν αὐτοῦ | LXX: λαμβάνω ("to take/receive") for nasa ("to carry/bear"); ἁμαρτίαν ("sin") for avon ("iniquity") — a slight semantic flattening of the Hebrew distinction between avon and chatah |

---

### Consistent Term Renderings

| Hebrew Term | LOGOS Rendering | Notes |
|-------------|-----------------|-------|
| YHWH | the LORD | Small capitals as conventional |
| nefesh | life | In vv. 11, 14 — biological/animating vitality, not Platonic soul; cf. note 4 |
| nefesh (personal) | person / person among you | In v. 12 legal formulation ("no person") |
| basar | flesh | Preserved throughout; physical/corporeal sense |
| dam | blood | Standard rendering |
| se'irim | goat-demons [se'irim] | Transliteration bracketed; demonic sense preserved; see note 3 |
| karet / nikrat | cut off | Standard legal formula for covenant excision |
| zevach shelamim | peace offerings | Formal equivalence tradition; shelamim = completeness/wholeness |
| olah | burnt offering | Consistent throughout Leviticus |
| kipper | make atonement | Pi'el consistently rendered with full phrase |
| chuqqat olam | a statute forever | Permanent-statutory formula |
| ha-ezrach | the native / native-born | Consistent with Lev 1–16 |
| ha-ger ha-gar | the sojourner who sojourns | Preserves the cognate verbal construction |
| ohel moed | tent of meeting | Consistent throughout Leviticus |
| mizbe'ach | altar | Standard rendering |
| nevelah | what has died naturally | Defined rather than transliterated on first occurrence |
| treifah | what was torn by beasts | Defined with agent specified |
| avon | iniquity | Distinguished from chatah (sin) |
| nasa avon | bear his iniquity | Standard formula throughout Leviticus |
| zanah | play the harlot | Formal register; covenant apostasy metaphor preserved |
| bney Yisra'el | sons of Israel | Formal rendering throughout |
| mishkan | tabernacle | In v. 4: "tabernacle of the LORD" — mishkan YHWH |

---

— LOGOS
